title: Positive Lyapunov exponents and mixing in stochastic fluid flow. Part II
speaker: Elia Bruè
abstract: In this three-part lecture series, we will present a series of works by Bedrossian, Blumenthal and Punshon-Smith on the chaotic mixing and enhanced dissipation properties of a passive tracer subject to the motion of an ergodic Markovian flow of spatially regular incompressible velocity fields on a compact domain. Such flows crucially include the motion of a velocity field solving the stochastic Navier-Stokes equations in T2 (or T3 with hyperviscosity), but can be more general or much simpler in character.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic Navier–Stokes Lagrangian model
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the two-dimensional stochastic Navier–Stokes equation on the torus with additive mode noise, regular solution space H^sigma, and the associated Lagrangian flow whose derivative will be studied.

proof_stages.label: Positive Lyapunov exponent theorem and stretching consequences
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts a deterministic positive top Lyapunov exponent for the derivative of the flow, a stronger lower bound for every direction, and exponential growth consequences for transported scalar gradients.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity-versus-exclusion proof roadmap
proof_stages.summary: The proof is organized into an abstract rigidity step using the multiplicative ergodic theorem and Furstenberg theory, followed by a stochastic Navier–Stokes-specific argument ruling out the resulting rigid structure.

proof_stages.label: Random dynamical system and linear cocycle abstraction
proof_stages.summary: The Lagrangian process is recast as a random dynamical system on Z=H times T^2 with a cocycle property, its derivative is viewed as an SL(2) linear cocycle, and stationary measures are related to the deterministic skew product.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicative ergodic theorem and Furstenberg projective rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Under integrability and ergodicity the multiplicative ergodic theorem gives a constant top exponent, and Furstenberg’s theorem says that if this exponent vanishes then a deterministic equivariant family of measures on projective space is preserved by the cocycle.

proof_stages.label: Matrix-law degeneracy and finite-dimensional exclusion strategy
proof_stages.summary: The invariant projective family implies that conditional laws of derivative matrices lie in empty-interior subsets of SL(2), so smooth positive transition densities would contradict zero exponent, as in finite-dimensional Hörmander settings.

proof_stages.label: Strong Feller upgrade in the infinite-dimensional problem
proof_stages.summary: For the full infinite-dimensional stochastic Navier–Stokes equation, the replacement for finite-dimensional density arguments begins with a strong Feller property of the Lagrangian Markov semigroup, which upgrades the Furstenberg invariant family from measurable to continuous.

proof_stages.label: Topological Furstenberg alternatives
proof_stages.summary: Using continuity and the structure of subgroups of SL(2), the zero-exponent rigidity is refined into two topological alternatives: a continuous invariant family of scalar products or a continuous finite family of lines permuted by the linear cocycle.

examples.name: Unforced Navier–Stokes when the noise set is empty
examples.note: The speaker notes that if the forced mode set K were empty the model would reduce to deterministic Navier–Stokes, illustrating why a nondegeneracy assumption on the noise is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: it is only cited as the degenerate case k empty, standard unforced Navier–Stokes, for which no result is provided

passes.reason: The empty noise set is the collapsed/minimal forcing case and is cited as the excluded obstruction to the theorem.
The empty noise set is the zero/minimal forcing boundary, and without forcing the positive-Lyapunov conclusion can fail, motivating the nondegeneracy assumption.

examples.name: Finite-dimensional Stokes and Galerkin Navier–Stokes variants
examples.note: These variants illustrate the contrast with the infinite-dimensional problem: in finite dimension one can use Furstenberg plus Hörmander-type density arguments, whereas the full stochastic Navier–Stokes case needs additional work.
examples.engagement_reason: the finite-dimensional Stokes/Galerkin variants are described as settings where one can use Furstenberg/Hörmander-type arguments, but no concrete verification or computation is carried out for them

passes.reason: The Stokes/Galerkin versions are reduced finite-dimensional truncations used as easier comparison models.

examples.name: Passive scalar transport equation
examples.note: The linear transport equation is used as an application of exponential stretching, showing that gradients of transported scalars grow exponentially in suitable norms.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the flow representation to the transport equation and derives exponential growth of scalar gradients from the Lyapunov result

passes.reason: The passive scalar transport equation is a standard linear advection model used as an application of the stretching result.

examples.name: Diagonal and anti-diagonal subgroup of SL(2)
examples.note: The subgroup generated by diagonal hyperbolic matrices together with axis-swapping anti-diagonal matrices makes concrete the noncompact alternative in which a finite family of lines is permuted.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes this subgroup’s diagonal and anti-diagonal elements, identifies the coordinate subspaces, and explains how the elements fix or swap them

passes.reason: The diagonal/anti-diagonal subgroup is a standard noncompact subgroup preserving the two coordinate lines and used only to illustrate the permutation alternative.
The diagonal/anti-diagonal subgroup is a proper reducible monomial subgroup preserving and swapping the two coordinate axes, used only to illustrate the noncompact rigid alternative.
